column5: Thomas Hart Benton, born Neosho M) 1889, died Kansas City, MA 1975.  Achelous and Hercules, 1947.  tempera and oil on canvas mounted on plywood.  "Benton saw the legend as a parable of his beloved Midwest. The Army Corps of Engineers had begun efforts to control the Missouri River, and Benton imagined a future when the waterway was tamed and the earth swelled with robust harvests."  Also may reflect Marshall plan, as American agriculture saved Europe, and legendary stubbornness of people of Missouri, like Hercules, "tough and strong," with "a reputation for doing what he thought was right."

column5: Synagogue, Ostia.  Main hall.  Discovered in 1961 at the edge of the town, near the ancient coastline, it Dates to the fourth century CE, though the excavations revealed two earlier phases of the building, the first of which Dates to the middle of the first century CE.   In its original construction, the synagogue consisted of a main hall with benches along three walls; a propyleum or monumental gateway with four marble columns; and a triclineum or dining room with three couches. A well with attached basin rested outside the entryway.  The main entrance of the synagogue faces the southeast, towards Jerusalem.

column5: Baker’s Tomb. Tomb of Eurysaces.  The tomb of Eurysaces the contract baker, showing an oven; Porta Maggiore, Rome.  Located in the fork of the Via Praenestina and the Via Labicana, just beyond the corssing of the Aqua Claudia.  The tomb was later built into the Porta Praenestina of Aurelian. column5: Porticus of Octavia, Rome.  built ostensibly by Octavia, the sister of Augustus (Fest. 178; Ov. AA i.69), but really by Augustus and dedicated in the name of Octavia (Suet. Aug. 29; Cass. Dio xlix.43; Liv. Ep. 138) at some time after 27 B.C.  Besides the temples, there were within the enclosure a biblioteca erected by Octavia in memory of the youthful Marcellus (Suet. de gramm. 21; Plut. Marc. 30), a curia Octaviae (Plin. NH xxxvi.28), and a schola (ib. xxxv.114) or scholae (ib. xxxvi.22).  Whether these were different parts of one building, or entirely different structures, is uncertain. column5: Amphitheater at Trier.  View from above.  About 100 A.D.  On the outskirts of the town.  It was richly decorated during the following centuries.  Its western half held the imperial box and seats reserved for high ranking civil servants.  Its main entrances, to the north and south, originally featured magnificent facades.   The arena had room for roughly 20000 spectators.  In the 4th and 5th centuries the Amphitheater was at the same time a town gateway, so that the north entrance lay inside and the south entrance outside the town walls.

column5: Imperial Thermae, Trier.  Late Antique Baths, view from across a modern street.  First half of the fourth century.  The hot-water baths (caldarium), a hall containing a huge semicircular pool on the long east wall of the rectangular building, and two smaller pools along the shorter wall are the best preserved part of the Thermae.  The network of corridors in the building's basement were necessary for the maintenance of the heating system and for the drainage of the waste-water from the baths, while at the same time providing access to the stair towers and light shafts.

column5: Porta Nigra.  Trier. Close up of one of the two towers flanking the enormous gate.  This enormous gate was built of the distinctive light sandstone, by placing one row of huge blocks on top of another, and then joining them with iron clamps without using any mortar. 2nd century A.D.  Its height is 30 m and its depth 22 m, while its front measures 36 m in length. It has 2 carriage ways, each 7 m high, leading through a windowless ground floor. There are 144 windows total with Roman arches on the first and second floors at the front, and looking across the inner court, provided with portcullis and secured by heavily strengthened gates. The two towers flanking the Porta Nigra, while built flush with the rest of the walls inside the courtyard, jutted outwards in massive curves at the front, where they faced the open fields, and were linked with the high walls (7 m) surrounding the town, covering an area of 28500 acres.

column3: Teatro Olympico of Palladio (Andrea di Pietro della Gondola, 1508-80) in Vicenza, Italy
column5: Teatro Olympico of Palladio (Andrea di Pietro della Gondola, 1508-80) in Vicenza, Italy. Exterior view. Constructed between 1580 and 1584, it opened after his death with a 1585 performance of Sophocles' Oedipus Rex. Perfectly preserved, the semicircular indoor theater recalls the design of ancient Greek venues.
